title: Roman chamomile soy jar candle
subtitle: A 200 ml container candle with roman chamomile and lavender essential oils
excerpt: Roman chamomile essential oil has a distinctive apple-sweet floral character that softens to something gentler than its Germanic cousin. At 5.4% of wax weight, it dominates this soy jar candle; lavender at 2.6% rounds out the blend. Roman chamomile is expensive, so this recipe uses it as the featured note rather than cutting it heavily.
sourceNotes: Soy wax container candle technique: Candlescience wick sizing guide (2023); CD-12 for 6 to 7 cm jar diameter. Fragrance load at 8% by wax weight: maximum for GW 464 soy wax. Roman chamomile EO (Anthemis nobilis or Chamaemelum nobile): apple-sweet, floral, mild. Distinct from German chamomile (Matricaria chamomilla), which is blue and more strongly medicinal-herbaceous. Note: roman chamomile is expensive; the lavender in this recipe extends it while keeping the chamomile as the dominant note.

recipe.yieldDescription: 1 x 200 ml jar candle (130 g soy wax fill)
recipe.batchNotes: Scale weights by the number of jars. One pre-tabbed wick per jar.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Cure for 3 to 5 days before burning. The apple-sweet character of roman chamomile develops during the cure.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Wax stable for 5 years or more. Roman chamomile and lavender are both relatively stable in wax; the blend should remain recognisable for 2 years. Keep the lid on between burns.

glossaryTerms.definition: The temperature at which liquid wax is poured into the container. Soy container wax is poured at 55 to 65°C; too hot and the wax pulls away from the sides; too cool and it sets unevenly.

The first burn of a candle sets the wax memory: the melt pool diameter on the first burn becomes the maximum it will reach on subsequent burns. Burning to the full jar diameter on the first burn prevents tunnelling.

content.text: Roman chamomile is not the same oil as German chamomile. German chamomile is blue with a strong, medicinal character; roman chamomile is pale yellow, apple-sweet, and floral. It is one of the more expensive essential oils, so this recipe uses it as the lead note at 5.4% of wax weight and pairs it with lavender at 2.6% to stretch the blend. The result is a soft, floral candle with an apple sweetness on the cold throw and in the early burn.

items.prepNote: Container-blend soy wax; GW 464 or equivalent
5.4% of wax weight; the featured note; apple-sweet, floral
2.6% of wax weight; extends and softens the chamomile
CD-12 for a 6 to 7 cm diameter jar

content.text: Place the soy wax in the candle pouring pitcher and set in a pan of barely simmering water. Heat, stirring occasionally, until fully melted and at 80°C. Remove from the heat and allow to cool.
Secure the sustainer tab of the pre-tabbed wick to the centre of the jar base with melted wax or a wick sticker. Thread the wick through the centring bar and lay the bar across the jar rim.
Add the essential oils and pour
When the wax has cooled to 60°C, add the roman chamomile and lavender essential oils and stir for 2 minutes. Pour at
: between 55 and 65°C. Pour in a slow stream down the side of the jar, leaving a 5 to 10 mm gap at the top. Leave undisturbed until fully set.
Cure for 3 to 5 days before burning. The apple-sweet quality of roman chamomile develops during the cure. Trim the wick to 5 mm before each burn.
. Burn until the melt pool reaches the full jar diameter (3 to 4 hours for a 200 ml jar). Do not burn in a draught.
Keep the lid on between burns. Wax stable for 5 years or more; roman chamomile and lavender both hold reasonably well in wax and the blend should remain recognisable for about 2 years.
